data.title: Big Tech, Breadth And Balance
data.description:
By Agatha Malinowski

U.S. equity markets have been whipsawed in the past few days, with initial optimism surrounding Big Tech earnings powering the S&P 500® to an intraday high, and subsequent disappointing reactions to earnings coupled with the Fed’s decision to hold rates steady leading to a sharp retreat for The 500®.

The pullback in Tech and the rotation toward small-cap stocks have been major market themes that have characterized the start of 2026.1 The S&P SmallCap 600® has outperformed The 500® by 4% month-to-date as of Jan. 28, 2026, propelled by strong economic growth and robust corporate earnings.

As the breadth of returns has started to expand beyond mega-cap leaders, it may be an interesting time to examine equal-weight strategies, which by design have a small-cap bias and offer a more broad-based footprint than their capitalization-weighted peers.

Exhibit 1 shows that the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index outperformed the capitalization-weighted S&P 500 by 3% over the three months ending Jan. 28, 2026, coinciding with a decline in market concentration, with the aggregate weight of The 500’s top 10 companies falling below 40% since Q3 2025.

Notably, market participation has expanded beyond the mega-cap names. Following the peak in concentration in early November, the sources of the S&P 500’s performance have shifted across the capitalization spectrum. Exhibit 2 shows that while the top 100 companies accounted for the majority of the index’s gains for most of 2025, mega-cap weakness has caused their contribution to decline considerably since then, as outperformance has shifted to the bottom 400 companies.

Sector-level dynamics further explain this shift. In a Brinson sector attribution of the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index versus the S&P 500, Exhibit 3 shows that the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index’s underperformance for most of last year and its subsequent outperformance relative to the S&P 500 was largely driven by an underweight in Information Technology, consistent with the sector’s recent travails.

The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index’s factor profile has also played a supportive role. In addition to its small-cap bias, the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index has historically maintained a persistent tilt toward value and away from momentum, as the strategy, by definition, regularly rebalances away from relative winners and toward relative losers. These factors have benefited from improving market breadth and a rotation away from large-cap growth- and momentum-oriented stocks. Notably, the S&P 500 Value has outperformed S&P 500 Growth, and the S&P 500 Momentum Index has underperformed The 500 by 2% so far in January.

Exhibit 4 shows that periods of extreme relative S&P 500 Momentum Index outperformance have often been followed by outperformance in the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index, reflecting the naturally inverse relationship between the two approaches. This historical trend may be particularly relevant following the S&P 500 Momentum Index’s stellar outperformance in 2025.

If the headwinds facing Big Tech continue to worsen, the increase in market breadth and the rotation away from large caps may potentially persist. We do know that periods following extreme market concentration have historically coincided with the outperformance of the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index, and it may be useful for market participants to understand the sources and drivers of the strategy’s relative performance.

1 The Wall Street Journal, “Small Stocks Finish Week at Records,” Jan. 16, 2026.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2026 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit www.spdji.com. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Whale's Insight: The Age Of Useful Crypto
data.description:
Davos 2026 Signals Crypto's Shift to Global Financial Infrastructure

At the World Economic Forum Annual Meeting in Davos 2026, crypto was framed no longer as a speculative fringe asset but as emerging core financial infrastructure. Discussion across panels focused on how blockchain, stablecoins, and tokenization can be embedded into payments, capital markets, banking, and even state-level economic strategy.

Tokenization of real-world assets dominated the agenda, with institutions such as BlackRock, BNY Mellon, and Euroclear showcasing pilots for tokenized bonds, funds, and deposits.

Stablecoins were positioned as the backbone of next-generation payments. Regulatory clarity in the U.S. was cited as the unlock for institutional scale, with pending legislation like the crypto market structure bill to accelerate TradFi participation.

Beyond finance, AI agents and blockchain were increasingly discussed as complementary systems, with crypto emerging as the preferred settlement layer for autonomous economic activity.

Key Take

  • Crypto adoption is being driven by institutions, not retail. The strongest signals from Davos came from asset managers, banks, and governments actively piloting tokenization and stablecoin infrastructure. The next phase of crypto adoption is being led by regulated incumbents who control capital, distribution, and compliance.

  • Regulatory clarity could trigger a structural market expansion. The dominant message for regulation is "clarity over perfection", and industry leaders argued that clear compliance pathways could break crypto's historical boom-bust cycle, enabling long-term capital formation.

  • AI-crypto integration is emerging as the next major demand driver. Beyond payments and tokenization, AI agents were repeatedly cited as new native users of blockchains. Crypto’s programmability, composability, and always-on settlement make it uniquely suited for autonomous machine-to-machine transactions, from paying for data and compute to executing economic decisions in real time.

Tether Launches U.S. Compliant Stablecoin USAT

Tether (USDT-USD), the issuer of the largest stablecoin USDT, has officially launched USAT, a dollar-backed stablecoin designed to operate within America's regulatory framework. Unlike, USDT, USAT is explicitly built for U.S. compliance following the passage of the GENIUS Act.

USAT will be issued through Anchorage Digital Bank, with Cantor Fitzgerald acting as reserve custodian and primary dealer. Former White House crypto policy advisor Bo Hines, now CEO of the Tether USAT, will oversee the rollout.

The launch comes amid accelerating institutional adoption of stablecoins, with Circle CEO Jeremy Allair projecting 40% annual growth as banks move beyond experimentation toward full-scale deployment.

Key Take

  • The U.S. stablecoin market is dominated by Circle's USDC (USDC-USD). Its competitive advantage lies in years of accumulated liquidity, trust, and large distribution channels. USDC is natively integrated across major exchanges, DeFi protocols, and banking partners. To compete, USAT needs to bootstrap liquidity and overcome lingering hesitation tied to Tether's offshore legacy. It is unlikely USAT can challenge USDC's market position in the near term.

  • Tether is pursuing a targeted, compliance-first distribution model rather than broad multichain and DeFi dominance. USAT's go-to-market focuses on regulated exchanges, banking partners, and U.S. Institutional channels. It intends to grow traditional finance and enterprise use cases first.

  • Right now, USDT already functions as the dominant onchain offshore dollar settlement layer across exchanges, emerging markets, and cross-border flows. USAT could be positioned as an on-ramp/off-ramp into that global USDT liquidity pool, enabling users to enter via a compliant U.S. stablecoin and settle internationally using USDT.

Ethereum to Launch AI Agents Standard on Mainnet

Ethereum (ETH-USD) announced a new standard, ERC-8004, which is designed to enable trustless AI agents on the network, is set to go live on the mainnet imminently. This standard defines a framework that allows AI agents to discover one another, build portable reputations, and interact with organizations and platforms on Ethereum.

It also introduces pluggable trust models, tiered by risk and security level, allowing agents to handle things from low-risk consumer tasks to high-stake use cases such as financial decision-making.

Technically, this new standard relies on three lightweight smart-contract registries: identity, reputation, and validation, enabling censorship-resistant agent identities, on-chain reputation via signed feedback, and verifiable validation of agent outputs.

Key Take

  • In addition to acting as a settlement layer for financial activities such as DeFi, stablecoin and tokenized assets, Ethereum is emerging as a trustless layer for AI agent coordination. The critical infrastructure - agent identity, reputation, and validation- enables AI-AI and AI-to-organization interactions.

  • As we outlined in our 2026 Outlook, Ethereum is facing increasing competition from both alternative Layer 1s and institution-supported new chains, which are fiercely competing on throughput and fees. This new AI agent settlement layer could provide Ethereum with a fresh new narrative that differentiates it from others.

  • 2026 is very likely to be an inflection point for AI agents, not because agents are now perfect, but because they are finally useful enough to deploy, iterate on, and build things. The recent surging interest in Clawbot is an early and concrete indication that AI agents are now crossing the boundary.

Weekly Market Chart: Tokenized Commodities Surge

Tokenized commodities have seen notable surges recently, particularly in the precious sector amid broader commodity rallies. The overall market cap for tokenized commodities has surpassed $4.5 billion, with transfer volumes increasing over 60%. This growth is largely driven by rising prices in gold and silver, which have repeatedly hit all-time highs.

Tether Gold (XAUT-USD) and Paxos Gold (PAXG-USD) dominate the sector, controlling over 90% of the tokenized gold supply. Other commodities like silver, copper, lithium, and uranium are part of the broader commodities rallies, but tokenized versions of them remain less prominent compared to gold.

Source: rwa.xyz

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Anavex And Alzheimer's Disease: The Future Never Seems To Be Now
data.description:
Anavex’s (AVXL) blarcamesine is one of those few Alzheimer’s drug candidates that has not failed, but has not succeeded either. It is thus stuck in perpetual limbo. My hold recommendation is based not on the anticipation that blarcamesine will escape this situation soon, but that it will eventually do so.

I will start with a brief history of Anavex 2-73/blarcamesine. At first, Anavex 2-73 appeared to be quite successful: it reportedly led to short-term improvements in those with mild cognitive impairment to mild Alzheimer’s disease and to long-term stabilization in these early-stage patients in a very small trial. However, these results were for subgroups, most notably for those with functioning sigma-1 receptors (table 3 and figure 2). In a much larger trial that lasted 48 weeks, blarcamesine significantly slowed down cognitive decline but did not significantly affect activities of daily living. Perhaps this is because the latter usually only subtly declines during the very early stages of the disease.

In the phase 2b/3 trial Anavex combined the 30 and 50mg groups, which led some critics to speculate that the company did this in order to cover up poor results in the 50mg group. When Anavex finally separated out the dose results, the 50mg group did do a little better. Anavex probably combined the groups because the statistical significance of the results had been diluted due to the high number of dropouts caused in part by dizziness and confusion.

In a 144 week open label extension trial, blarcamesine appeared to perform better than Aricept/donepezil and at least as well as the anti-amyloid drugs Leqembi and Kisunla, but without the risks of brain swelling and brain bleeds found in these later drugs (previous article). Blarcamesine also showed efficacy in regards to activities of daily living (results).

Anavex felt confident enough in these results to seek approval from European Medicines Agency (EMA), but was rebuffed. The Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use - - CHMP -- of the EMA expressed several objections and concerns regarding blarcamesine. Two of its main concerns were that blarcamesine did not demonstrate efficacy in regards to activities of daily living in the 48 week trial and that it had several side effects. CHMP also cited methodological problems but did not provide any details. The committee detected what it felt might be a new potential side effect: nitrosamine impurities that could potentially be a risk for cancer. Tetrahdyrofurans (blarcamesine is tetrahydrofuran derivative) and nitrosamine impurities (potentially linked to one of blarcamesine’s metabolites, succinaldehyde) may possibly be carcinogenic, but to the best of my knowledge, no increased risk of cancer has been found in Alzheimer’s patients taking blarcamesine.

Anavex stock has rebounded some over the last month or so based on three pieces of news: the company has asked CHMP to reevaluate its decision with a new set of scientists, it has begun working with the U.S. Food and Drug Administration as to a possible pathway to consideration, and it has a working agreement with the European Union’s Access-AD to test the drug in clinical trial settings.

None of these three strategies, though, is likely to avoid the necessity for at least one lengthy, large phase 3 clinical trial (18 months?, a 1,000 patients?). Anavex has suggested that dizziness and confusion can be reduced with nighttime dosing and a different titration schedule and that there is a significant slowing down in decline in activities of daily living over time, but the EMA probably will not accept these points on face value. The situation with the FDA may be somewhat analogous. Despite all the other turmoil surrounding health policy in the Trump administration, the FDA drug approval policy for neurological diseases does not seem to have substantially changed. While the FDA in the past has approved drugs based on ambiguous data and/or less than phase 3 trials, most such approvals have been controversial and relatively rare. And while the European Union Access-AD data may provide additional information as to whom most benefits from blarcamesine that likely cannot be substituted for a phase 3 clinical trial.

In the long run, blarcamesine may provide a better standard of care than Aricept because it is not only a sigma-1 receptor agonist like Aricept but also because, unlike Aricept, it may act as a direct antioxidant as a tetrahydrofuran derivative and through one of its metabolites – gamma hydroxybutyrate. Sigma-1 receptor agonists inhibit the breakdown of acetylcholine (a neurotransmitter needed for the retrieval of short-term memory) and partially maintain the synthesis of acetylcholine by reducing oxidation and nitration, but only for a relatively short while. Direct antioxidants would slow down the early progression of Alzheimer’s disease over a longer period of time.

Anavex has a cash flow of over one hundred million dollars and a cash runway of approximately three years (fourth quarter report). The company likely has the money to carry out future trials for Alzheimer's disease as well as other neurological indications, but the margin is not nearly as great as it once was.

If Anavex has to run a phase 3 clinical trial, the stock value will probably drop to about 2 points. Long-term investors will have to wait at least three more years to find out how effective and safe the drug actually is. The existing data and the mechanisms of action for blarcamesine suggest that the drug candidate is effective in those with a functioning sigma-1 receptor and that it is relatively safe. It is not a cure for the disease, but it may represent a significant improvement over existing drugs for the early stages of the disease. Given the current low stock value for Anavex (albeit it is likely going lower for quite some time), it may be worth holding until the final verdict is in.

data.title: Aker BP: Good Company, Tricky Short-Term Outlook
data.description:
In my last article on Aker BP, when the company was below 260 NOK, I gave the company a hold rating following the 3Q25 period. Aker BP is a solid Norwegian oil player. In the short term, the company dropped quite a bit after my article, which made the rating, in my view, the correct one.

You can see the short-term trend following November below.

So in this article, I'm going to look at the 2026E period for the company. Aker BP is a fundamentally great business. I don't see any long-term threats because of the company's low debt, excellent asset portfolio, and its being one of the primary energy players on the NCF, the continental shelf outside of Norway. It has a very high yield - even at this price it's above 9% and my YoC is closer to 11%, and it's one of the primary income generators for my portfolio.

The way that I invest in Aker BP is very simple. I've targeted a low-valuation entry PT of below 220-215 NOK, where I "BUY" shares (sometimes a bit higher). Meanwhile, when the company rises above 250 NOK, I'm careful. When it's above 265 NOK, I consider it to be a clear "HOLD", and when it's above 275 or 280 NOK, it becomes more of a rotation target due to the dangers implied by anything approaching 300 NOK. As I am writing the article, Aker BP has just risen above 280 NOK.

As an energy business, the company's main correlation is to oil and gas. This is a very volatile market - and even though Aker has some of the best production costs in the industry (in Europe/the West) and good returns. But Aker BP doesn't control the demand in its end markets. Major global oil players such as the Middle East, South America, Russia, and others have a significant impact on the prices for oil, and therefore for Aker BP. Plenty of times historically, there have been downsides for the company despite there being no company-specific operational reason for that downside.

So that's what I'm looking at next to company results, and as we move forward into 2026E.

Aker BP - Upside into 2026E is less clear than before at this price

Let me make it clear - at an attractive price, I'm perfectly willing and happy to not only invest in the company, but also keep it in my portfolio as an investment. However, energy companies like Aker BP are not suitable long-term investments, because the underlying commodity is not in itself stable. This can be reflected in the short-term expectations for oil prices in the coming fiscal year.

Every oil company forecasts its profits to some extent at various supposed forward oil price levels. When those go down, the profits go down. The "break even" point is different for many. Aker BP has a low break-even price, but despite this, a sub-$45/bbl oil price would hurt the company's results and the potential to risk-free pay its dividend.

And for 2026, the outlook for global Brent pricing is not good. This makes the article, or the thesis, not so much about Aker BP itself, but about the general trend in oil price averages.

The current forecast is a significant global surplus in oil. This is meaningful. Ignoring this and being heavily invested in these energy companies can be done only at your own short-term peril. Current forecasts imply $52/share, with some going lower, and some going higher. Impacts are coming, especially from non-Opec+ countries bringing capacity and supply to market.

Beyond that, I see two major risks to the development of oil prices. One, what the US will do in Venezuela. Bringing Venezuelan capacity back higher or to the market in general will increase the oversupply. Also, in the event of Russia and Ukraine ending the war, there's a chance of Russia's oil capacity being brought back to the market. In those scenarios, I see a potential for oil prices, without Opec+ intervention, to slide to $40/bbl - or even lower. Please note also that the US political administration has made it a key policy to reduce oil prices, which implies also lower pricing.

On the long side/bullish arguments, we find Opec+ intervention and geopolitical risk increase, which can point to an oil price recovery to about $76-$80/bbl in 2028E. The reason for this is that low pricing in 2026E will reduce energy CapEx. This tracks, but it's a volatile forecast.

As you can discern, there are a lot of moving parts here. How we handle this for our investments is therefore important. In my Aker BP investment, I am up more than 20% at this time, over 28% with the dividends that I've gotten during my time. It's a meaningful rate of return, and because of the company's valuation, there's now significant room for downward movement.

Recent results and forecasts for the full year do not spell out good trends. While Aker BP grows more efficiently, the current forecast for the full year is expected to come in below 10 NOK in AEPS. This doesn't matter all that much for the longer term. What matters here is the growth we're going to see from the company's significant and qualitative investments. In a positive energy backdrop, a double-digit annualized EPS growth rate of 20-40% until 2028E is entirely possible. But if something were to affect the company, say a downturn in energy pricing, we have a chance to see a repeat of some of the rather negative medium-term trends we've seen prior.

Anyone forecasting the company also needs to at least acknowledge the fact that any attempt at forecasting here is done only at a high risk. S&P Global's forecast accuracy for a 1-year basis with a 10% margin of error is exactly 0%. That means that 100% of the time, the company misses forecasts either positively or negatively (60%+) of the time negatively.

When a company has these sorts of fundamental trends, this is, to me, a sign to be conservative and perhaps more short-term oriented.

The point is, Aker BP manages a break-even oil price that's solid - about $35-$40/bbl for most of its portfolio. But in the event of certain geopolitical realities, that oil price no longer seems to be that far off, with possibilities of sub-$50/share being on the table.

Even if I don't view this possibility as a major risk that would make the company "invalid" as an investment, due to superb fundamentals, I at the very least believe this to possibly result in a significant share price drop.

Because I am a valuation-oriented investor, that's something I have to consider and react to.

To clarify, the company's 4Q25 earnings and full-year results are bound to be presented within a 2-week period. However, the risks I see in this article are primarily macro-focused, not Aker-focused or operational. Because of this, I don't believe that the individual variances for actual versus forecasted 2025 results really matter - unless the variance turns out to be major (which I doubt). Even if it's a major upside variance and the stock rises on earnings day, this would only enhance and confirm my decision to now view Aker BP as a short-term rotation target due to significant valuation.

This is the reason why the results for the near term do not impact this thesis or my view on the company.

Aker BP - Upside is tricky, protecting capital is now becoming more of a priority.

Going into 2026 in an overvalued market (which I believe we have here), I put a significantly higher importance on protecting gains and capital increases, while preventing downside. This demands of me that I sell/rotate positions, even though there's a risk that the position may rise further. This is especially true of large positions, which Aker BP happens to be (over 3% of my portfolio). The potential for upside, or the company's superb qualities that I have often spoken about in my previous articles, do not change my view on this one bit.

You will note that I put my previous PT at 240 NOK. I'm cautiously lowering it to 235 NOK - but note that some analysts have it as low as 150 NOK (though I wouldn't take that all that seriously personally). I'm saying that the potential upside is very low at 280+ NOK, and what you're left with is the yield. The yield is not unattractive - but it's also not necessarily enough, when you're trying to defend over 50% annualized gains.

If I were you, I'd view my role in my portfolio as the general or commander of a battle. You allocate your resources, and you advance where it's justified, and you retreat/back off where the signs or facts point to that being the best choice. In this context and situation, the short-term 2026-2027E implications for energy are not good, in my view. I therefore now make it a point to consider rotation for any position in oil/energy where I have the room to do so.

Most oil companies trade at conservative 10-15x P/E multiples. Some lower. It's difficult to forecast Aker BP with straight AEPS. It's also worth noting that Aker BP, for many, many years, was unprofitable in terms of AEPS. Deeply negative.

The uncertainty in valuation is expressed through the variety of price targets that even something like a DCF or DDM models. You may consider my update for 2026E to be excessively bearish on the company and ignoring some of the company-specific upsides, in favor of sector-negative trends. There's some truth to that. Note, however, that my position is based on an entry below 220 NOK on average. These "gains" are neither irrelevant nor something I'm willing to easily abandon. If it were possible to more easily and more reliably forecast the company, I would probably be able to allow for some more upside, maybe above 300-330 NOK. But the way this company has moved, and moves, it's very difficult to know just where it will go. Some risks I will mention below make this trend even harder.

Therefore, as we enter 2026E, I consider Aker BP to be a "HOLD", and now as of the 29th of January, a rotation target due to the price rising above 280 NOK. For myself, I am seriously considering rotation due to the profit that I've been able to make in the business. I lower my PT slightly to about 235 NOK to reflect increasingly problematic end-market trends. This represents about a 24x 2025E P/E, and a 15x 2026-2027E one. My fair value target range of 280-350 NOK goes to above 18-20x 2026-2027E, whereas some analysts forecasting this company at 30x go to above 500-600 NOK.

For AKRBY, the ADR I typically prefer, this PT of 235 NOK comes to about $12.25/share.

Risks are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

As I said in my previous article on the company, investing in oil is a fertile "soil" for dividends and conservative valuation upside - provided you have both the patience and acumen to recognize undervaluation and overvaluation. The profit from Aker BP thus far has been impressive, but based on operational results, financial fundamentals, and current oil market trends, I believe the time has come not only for a "HOLD", but for a serious rotation consideration. Risks now suddenly include political ones as well, with a recent potential Supreme Court case in Norway calling into question some of the approvals that have been given. If the ruling here was to mirror that from the lower court, some of the company's projects would be in trouble. The ruling is set to be a plenary one, and while the history is positive (in 2020 the Supreme Court ruled against environmental interests in a similar one), the ruling by the lower court is worrying. This is also to be considered in light of an ECtHR case, where the European court "backed off" a bit from one of its earlier climate judgements - but not entirely.

The main non-legal and macro risk that I see is a reopening of Russia, in terms of macro. If this happens, I expect oil prices, at least short-term, would drop to below $50 but above $40/bbl (in line with EIA forecasts), and this would be close to break-even pricing for Aker BP. This would not impact short-term dividends, but may impact longer term potentials.

Thesis

I consider Aker BP to be one of the most interesting oil exploration and production companies in all of Europe, and this has not changed in 2026 - even if some of the specifics have changed.

I still view Aker BP as having an upside to a conservative fair valuation target of 300 NOK, at least potentially. However, recent political changes and macro changes have added to the Russian return risk, as well as a Venezuelan oil risk. Combined with the already-confirmed oil surplus for 2026, I believe a slight lowering of my PT is in order. Combine this with the legal risks as well as the short-term volatility of the company, and I reiterate "HOLD" with a PT of 235 NOK, and also clearly now consider the company a rotation target here. This is because of a close to 30%+ dividend-inclusive and FX-inclusive rate of return for my position.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative and well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically is not enough. Based on this and a valuation target of 235 NOK/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that is attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Protagonist Therapeutics: Assessing The 2026 Upside For Icotrokinra And Rusfertide
data.description:
Thesis

Protagonist Therapeutics (PTGX) stock had been on an upward trend for most of 2025. We've seen the stock up about 115% on the 1-year chart as momentum for their two late-stage assets in icotrokinra and rusfertide has continued to grow. However, the stock peaked in October last year as there were reports that Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) was in talks to buy the company. I initiated my buy call back in my previous coverage and highlighted the attractiveness of the company. Since then, takeover speculation has remained quite flat, and the stock price has come down from the $95 high it hit back in mid-December. I think a lot of that stock momentum was due to the JNJ takeover speculation, and with current shares now trading in the $81 range, we should keep in mind the potential and upcoming catalysts Protagonist has in 2026.

3Q25

Back in early November, Protagonist reported some pretty solid, albeit investment-heavy 3Q25 results. We're clearly seeing the company transition from late-stage development toward potential commercialisation, as investors would hope. For the quarter, Protagonist reported license and collaboration revenue of about $4.7 million, a figure which was essentially flat year-over-year and driven mainly by their development services under the Takeda partnership. Operating expenses increased quite a bit with R&D rising to $40.0 million, up slightly from the $36.0 million we saw in 3Q24. It's a reflection of the expanded discovery and preclinical activity with the overall advancement of its pipeline. Elsewhere, G&A expenses climbed to $11.1 million as a result of higher professional services costs.

Protagonist for the quarter reported a much wider net loss of $39.3 million, or about -$0.62 per share, compared to a net loss of $33.2 million, or -$0.54 per share, in the prior-year quarter. Importantly, however, their cash position is still pretty strong, and it needs to be with the upcoming developments in 2026. They managed to end the quarter with a hefty sum of $678.8 million in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities, up considerably from $559.2 million at the end of FY24. Management has tipped for this figure to provide an expected cash runway until at least the end of 2028. In general, that increasing spending we're seeing is to support a couple of their late-stage/early-stage programs, as I'll explain now. However, the company is also trying to maintain as much financial flexibility as it can since its key assets in icotrokinra and rusfertide are rapidly approaching potential regulatory approvals this year, which is why I feel it is a good time to take an assessment of the stock.

The pipeline

As you know, Protagonist's pipeline is mostly anchored by its two late-stage programs approaching potential commercialization, both of which would be classed as very high-value in my opinion. Firstly, its lead asset, icotrokinra, is a first-in-class oral IL-23 receptor antagonist now being developed for moderate/severe plaque psoriasis. It already has New Drug Applications submitted in both the U.S. and Europe, with additional studies underway in inflammatory bowel disease indications like ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease. Now, the second advanced program would be rusfertide, which is a subcutaneous hepcidin mimetic mainly for the treatment of polycythemia vera but may be applicable to other related blood disorders. Rusfertide is currently in Phase 3 development and has received FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation. We can expect it to be submitted for U.S. approval by the end of this year if all goes well.

Elsewhere, just to touch briefly on their early pipeline candidates, Protagonist has initiated a Phase 1 trial of PN-881. This is a first-in-class oral IL-17 peptide antagonist currently being evaluated for the treatment of inflammatory and immune-mediated diseases. The company also has some metabolic and haematology candidates, including the triple GLP/GIP/GCG agonists PN-477sc and PN-477o for obesity and metabolic disease. These are in some IND-enabling studies, with an oral hepcidin program for iron regulation disorders also taking place. We can expect a development candidate to be nominated sometime this year.

Takeda partnership

Despite the other pipeline developments, I think it's fair to value Protagonist and the future stock movement on the upside of their two main candidates, rusfertide and icotrokinra. 2026 is going to be a big year for both assets, with multiple milestones to look out for. If we start with rusfertide, the candidate is a first-in-class investigational therapy, now being developed in partnership with Takeda for polycythemia vera (PV).

I think having Takeda in the same boat should be pretty beneficial going forward. Even though Takeda will hold global rights to commercialise rusfertide outside the United States, they're going to play a major potential role in global marketing and launch activities. They have an extensive rare disease and haematology commercial infrastructure to leverage. And in the U.S., Takeda and Protagonist are set up as co-commercialisation partners, so they have a negotiated profit-sharing arrangement, with Protagonist still retaining the right to opt out of the 50:50 split for any enhanced milestone and royalty terms. The protagonist also remains the one primarily responsible for completing Phase 3 development and securing regulatory approval, so before we discuss the market, investors would hope that 2026 is a positive year for the candidate.

Rusfertide

Now, PV is actually a pretty rare chronic blood disorder in which we often see the bone marrow overproducing red blood cells (erythrocytosis). As you can imagine, this can lead to all kinds of problems, including elevated hematocrit, increased blood viscosity, and a higher risk of clots/thrombotic events. Mechanistically speaking, rusfertide can essentially mimic the natural hormone hepcidin, which is the body's key regulator of iron homeostasis. So, in presenting a mimic form, this can bind to ferroportin and then reduce iron export into the bloodstream. The goal here is for rusfertide to limit overall iron availability for red blood cell production, in theory lowering hematocrit and reducing any need for frequent therapeutic phlebotomies/blood draws that are the big mainstay of PV management.

So far in clinical development, rusfertide has shown us pretty strong efficacy with a consistent safety profile across multiple studies. The Phase 3 VERIFY study is a global, randomised, placebo-controlled trial in PV patients who remained phlebotomy-dependent despite the current standard of care. So these patients keep requiring regular therapeutic blood removal procedures to help manage their disease even though they are already being treated with some currently approved medications like as hydroxyurea or interferons. Now, the study met its primary endpoint and also hit all four key secondary endpoints, which was a good sign. It showed substantial and durable hematocrit control and, importantly, marked reductions in phlebotomy eligibility through to Week 32. It also sustained this all the way until Week 52, with many patients actually maintaining target hematocrit levels below 45%. The VERIFY data is going to be extremely important in regulatory scrutiny since it also showed improvements in patient-reported outcomes. In general, they experienced less fatigue and symptom burden, with a manageable safety profile mostly dominated by mild injection-site reactions.

Elsewhere, to accelerate regulatory progress, rusfertide has received U.S. FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation, which was a nice stock catalyst adding to the momentum. In addition, it was also awarded Orphan Drug designation and Fast Track status, which goes to show its potential to offer a massive improvement over available treatments since reducing the need for blood withdrawals is a huge benefit. The NDA was submitted earlier this month, and based on the robust Phase 3 results, I think we can expect a potential approval and subsequent commercialisation from Takeda sometime in 2026/2027, pending regulatory review. I also think an approval here would mark signs of a potential shift in PV care away. Moving more away from burdensome phlebotomy, which no one likes, toward a targeted biologic therapy.

Icotrokinra

When it comes to icotrokinra, again, it is a first-in-class investigational therapy. However, unlike rusfertide, which is a subcutaneous injection, icotrokinra is a targeted oral peptide. Now, icotrokinra is specifically designed to selectively block the interleukin-23 (IL-23) receptor, a key driver of inflammatory signalling in a quite a few immune-mediated diseases like moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis and also some inflammatory bowel diseases. The aim of icotrokinra here is to bind to the IL-23 receptor on T cells with pretty high affinity and interrupt the IL-23 signalling pathway. You see, this pathway is tipped to contribute to chronic inflammation and skin lesion formation, so it can offer a targeted mechanism distinct from many currently used systemic therapies.

If we start with plaque psoriasis, Protagonist has its pivotal Phase 3 ICONIC clinical program, which has delivered some rather robust results. In the ICONIC-LEAD trial, a once-daily treatment of icotrokinra achieved significant and durable skin clearance compared to the placebo. What's more, is that the majority of patients reached Investigator's Global Assessment 0/1, which basically means clear or almost clear skin. There was also a recorded PASI 90 figure by Week 16, which means a greater than 90% improvement on the Psoriasis Area and Severity Index. And even higher response rates were seen at Week 24, which suggests that a substantial proportion managed to achieve completely clear skin. All this was also recorded with a favourable safety profile similar to placebo. Elsewhere, the ICONIC-TOTAL study also met its primary endpoint in patients with psoriasis affecting particularly difficult-to-treat areas like the scalp and genital regions. In addition to this, the Phase 3 ICONIC-ADVANCE 1 & 2 results showed us icotrokinra's superiority versus an active comparator in deucravacitinib, which should add further strength to the efficacy profile across the program.

Now, beyond just dermatology, icotrokinra has generated particular investor interest since it has been tipped to show some promise in ulcerative colitis (UC). The most important data relating to this would be the Phase 2b ANTHEM-UC study, where we saw it meet the primary clinical response endpoints. It showed improvements in clinical remission and endoscopic findings versus the placebo. Now, this should help support its potential in inflammatory bowel disease; however, for near term catalysts, I think we can be mainly focused on its use in psoriasis.

All in all, it's a pretty comprehensive clinical dataset from multiple Phase 3 studies. An NDA has already been submitted to the U.S. FDA, with regulatory submissions in Europe now underway. So, as for now, icotrokinra is mainly set up for possible approval as an oral systemic treatment for both adults and adolescents who have moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis.

Combined TAM

When it comes to the market for their two main candidates, I think it's best to look at both combined since both assets are now in late-stage development. Again, if we start with rusfertide, the market for PV treatment is expanding pretty rapidly since quite a few other novel therapies have entered the space. Estimates, of course, vary by source, but the market was valued around $7.1 billion back in 2025 and is projected to grow rather significantly over the coming decade, with some forecasts hitting a hefty $13.8 billion by 2032. The market is said to be mainly driven by increased diagnosis and new targeted treatments. Now, within this space, there are already some existing approved therapies, which indicate there is a significant pricing premium for patients. For example, ruxolitinib/Jakafi has a wholesale acquisition cost of about $18,000 per bottle, but the actual annual therapy costs are dependent on dosing and insurance. There is also ropeginterferon alfa-2b or BESREMi, which is reported to exceed $180,000 annually in the U.S. These are some massive pricing dynamics, and because of the chronic nature of PV, along with rusfertide's novel mechanism. I think we could be looking at something similar. Keep in mind that the therapy has some potential to reduce phlebotomy dependence, which I expect Takeda to focus on should the drug eventually get marketed.

Icotrokinra, on the other hand, will sit in the broader psoriasis market, which is pretty large and well-established. The current global psoriasis therapy sales are tipped to exceed $30 billion in key markets. However, within that, moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis, which is the principal indication for icotrokinra that investors can expect, is a major segment we frequently see get treated with systemic biologics and small molecules. The current IL-23 and related targeted therapies, as you would expect, carry quite high annual costs. For example, skyrizi/risankizumab lists at about $23,800 per dose and is usually administered multiple times per year. There are also quite a few TYK2 inhibitors like sotyktu/deucravacitinib, which have historically had annual costs in the $14,000 range. But keep in mind that variations in price exist depending on strategies and discounts. Specifically, some analysts have suggested that novel oral agents could capture a pretty significant share of the injectable biologics since they offer a far more convenient dosing regimen and competitive efficacy.

Valuation risk

If you take a glance at Protagonist's valuation page, the stock screens as pretty expensive. I think it's a sign of its status as a late-stage company with limited recurring revenue and significant stock dependence on future product approvals from two potential candidates. As a result, it makes traditional earnings metrics largely uninformative. With a market cap of about $5.1 billion and a hefty cash sum of about $576 million, this gives us an enterprise value nearing $4.4 billion. This would indicate that most of the current valuation is being driven by expectations for icotrokinra and rusfertide rather than current operations. The stock also sees quite a healthy bit of trading activity for a biopharma stock, with the average daily trading volume recently around 777,000 shares over the past year.

On a revenue basis, the stock trades at a very elevated multiple, with a trailing EV to sales ratio of about 21x, well above the healthcare sector median, which goes to show how modest the company's collaboration revenue is relative to its market value. It's a premium valuation, which suggests investors are pricing in a lot of future commercial success from those two late-stage assets. Keep in mind also that the stock has heavily outperformed the S&P over the last year, and there are still massive expectations priced into this stock, dependent on regulatory approval of both icotrokinra and rusfertide. So there's clearly some risk here in that I see more upside to come in 2026 based on the trajectory of these two candidates, but negative outcomes would mean that momentum can get priced out rather quickly.

Looking ahead elsewhere, key milestones

As for Protagonist's near-term milestones, investors should look out for the advancement of some of their early-stage programs, including the PN‑881 Phase 1 results and IND-enabling studies for PN‑477 candidates. We can expect those in 2026, along with the nomination of an oral hepcidin candidate by year-end. The company's 4Q25 earnings are set to fall sometime in mid-March, also.

data.title: Viemed Healthcare: Remaining Bullish On Keen Valuation And Buoyant Cash Flow
data.description:
Intro

Our last coverage on Viemed Healthcare, Inc. (VMD) was in April of last year, when we reiterated our bullish stance in the home medical equipment and respiratory healthcare provider. Rising equity, bullish EPS expectations, and a keen valuation were key reasons why we maintained our bullish rating at the time. Although EPS revisions have been somewhat disappointing over the past 30 days, the 41% growth projection aligns with what was expected back in April of last year. Furthermore, with the stock reporting only an approximate 12% gain over the past nine months, further upside looks on the cards for VMD, given how the company has improved over the first three quarters of fiscal 2025.

Q1 Earnings Much Better From A Seasonal Standpoint

Net income of $2.6 million in Q1 (Announced 5/8/2025) was a substantial improvement over the $1.6 million number in the same period of 12 months prior. Operating cash flow also experienced a significant jump to come in at $2.9 million. Improved profitability came as a result of much higher revenues ($59.1 million) in the quarter, resulting in a 26% spike in adjusted EBITDA ($12.8 million). Furthermore, the encouraging trends (from a forward-looking basis) were the 13% rolling-quarter growth rate in the ventilator patient count and the 46% increase in the company's PAP therapy patient count in the quarter. Given that Viemed's patients are essentially 'assets' that produce consistent revenues, it wasn't surprising to see Viemed lift the lower end of its revenue guidance. Furthermore, given that Q1 from a seasonal standpoint is traditionally Viemed's worst quarter, these types of growth trends certainly bode well for both Q2 and Q3.

Q2 Earnings Reports Strong Growth In Addressed Patient Population

Before Viemed announced its second-quarter earnings for fiscal 2025. We received notice in early July that the Lehan's Medical Equipment purchase had gone through. Lehan's is an Illinois based family outfit that focuses on women's medical equipment. Given the $26 million approximate purchasing price and $2 million+ of contingency payments, the deal certainly looked good on the surface, given Lehan reported well in excess of $7 million in adjusted EBITDA in 2024.

Viemed's GAAP profit of $3.2 million in Q2 this year surpassed the corresponding GAAP number in Q1 of 2024 by a significant margin ($1.5 million). Furthermore, what investors need to understand here is that the additional guidance hike was not solely based on the additions from Lehan, but on strong organic gains in Viemed over multiple quarters now. These fundamentals are directly tied to the company's growing patient population and continued success in the likes of Viemed's sleep and vent business. Shares rallied aggressively, well north of $7 on the release of the report, but have failed to rally above this point since then (Q2 earnings released 8/6/2025) as we see below.

FY2025 Top-Line Guidance Gets Trimmed, But Strong Fundamentals Remain

Viemed missed its top and bottom-line estimates in Q3, although the top-line result ($719 million) was a record for the company, on the back of strong organic growth and Lehan's additions. It was impressive to see how quickly Lehan's numbers became accretive to Viemed's bottom line ($3.5 million), given the acquisition only closed a few months previously. Although full-year top-line guidance was cut to approximately the $272 million level, we believe management was being cautious here due to the limited time remaining in fiscal 2025 at that juncture.

Shareholder-Friendly Management On View

Management teams that lower expectations are always welcome in this day and age, as this is usually the minority, until companies are forced to do so. Other trends we like concerning the management make-up in Viemed are the high insider ownership (14%+), rising equity and strong buyback volumes (1.7+ million shares alone bought back in Q3). Ultimately, the above trends are extremely shareholder-friendly as they facilitate robust levels of bottom-line growth over time (Which the market wants to see to upgrade the stock significantly). The CEO laid the above premise out in his Q3 remarks, cited below

We delivered another outstanding quarter marked by strong execution and sustained expansion of our patient base across every major service line. Our disciplined approach to capital deployment was demonstrated through the full completion of our 2025 share repurchase program, while the acquisition of Lehan's Medical Equipment further enhanced our market presence and diversified our service capabilities. Both initiatives were accretive to earnings per share and reflect our unwavering commitment to creating long-term shareholder value while improving patient outcomes across the communities we serve

Cheap Valuation

Therefore, we are unduly worried about recent consolidation, as strong cash-flow trends will continue to pave the way for more organic and inorganic growth as well as aggressive buybacks. In essence, as long as sufficient sales and earnings are generated, which produce positive cash flow, then this very same invested cash simply has to drive the stock north over time, all things remaining equal. We state this because Viemed's P/B, P/S, P/E, and P/C remain well below their 5-year averages, and ROC has grown. This makes us expect some type of reversion in this play sooner rather than later.

Metric (Trailing) VMD (Trailing) VMD 5-Year Average
Price To Earnings (GAAP) 21.73 29.90
Price To Sales 1.13 1.82
Price To Book 2.10 2.78
Price To Cash-Flow 5.79 8.66
Return On Capital 8.2% 8.17%

Risks

Given the level of investment Viemed has committed to in recent times, the company is banking on demand remaining elevated, where patient numbers continue to grow aggressively. Remember, the success of Viemed's sales personnel in the Vent and Sleep businesses will be determined to a large degree by the purchasing power of the end customer. Therefore, with the US dollar having weakened pretty significantly in recent sessions, a continuation of this trend could result in growing inflation, which may dampen end-user demand in fiscal 2026 and beyond.

Furthermore, as mentioned, although Viemed has a strong insider ownership position and excellent balance sheet, the micro-cap nature of the stock (and the below-average trading volume this entails) means breaches of support levels can take place more easily than in larger companies. Therefore, binary events in particular must be watched, such as the company's upcoming Q4 earnings report set to be released in early March.

Conclusion

Therefore to sum up, we are reiterating our 'Buy' rating in VMD due to growing revenues, earnings, and cash flow, strong expansion of patient base; and cheap valuation. Let's see what Q4 numbers bring in early March. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Legrand: Updating For 2026E After Lackluster Performance In Q3 2025
data.description:
In this article, I will provide an update on my previous thesis for data center and digital real estate/infrastructure business Legrand SA (LGRDY). It's fair, in my view, to call Legrand a qualitative industrial player with significant attractive upside potential at the right price. As I said in my last piece, however, that price was not at the time.

Time has at least partially proven me to be correct. Since my last article, the company has underperformed the overall indices, and since the underperformance is more than 5% in total, I view this as beyond a mere statistical aberration. Specifically, what we're seeing is the downside and challenges following the 3Q period, which has been impacting the business.

In this article, I'll update my thesis on Legrand and show you why I believe in my updated 2026E targets for the company. I have not yet bought Legrand, nor do I intend to do so at this particular time - there are other undervalued companies that currently command my attention and my capital.

Also, and beside this, I view the current market as being significantly overvalued in every sense of the word. While there may be undervalued businesses, I believe even the businesses that I invest in risk despite their valuation to drop further in the event of a market crash. A market crash, which I believe, trends point to. Everything from extreme P/E valuations (26x+), Shiller CAPE (over 39, implied historical resets if we look at the past 100 years), a Hyper-bull sentiment signaling extreme overconfidence, and the like. More than anything, though, and because I'm a fundamental analyst at heart, I'm worried about ongoing quantitative tightening with high interest rates, not only in the US but in Europe as well. This means that the level of liquidity in the system overall is very limited, and institutions such as banks are becoming more careful (as we can see in Sweden on a day-to-day basis with lending restrictions).

Aside from this, there's the obvious capital concentration in AI - which Legrand, in an indirect way, is a part of.

Let's see what we have here.

Legrand - Upside is limited, unless the significant AI-related and tech growth continues

To be clear, I don't have a forecast for if and when a crash would begin or come. It's always hard to tell. I remember sitting at the trading desk during the worst days in COVID-19 over 5 years ago. Investors and analysts around me, and online, were all panicking (or at least giving a good impression of it). Me, because I was prepared, I bought. I expect I will do the same if and when the market crashes again.

Legrand's significant double-digit drop on earnings day was because of a clear earnings miss. I won't say that I clearly forecasted such an earnings miss, but it was definitely part of my thesis at the time. Furthermore, I believe the likelihood of more downward weight or potential to be high.

Legrand's 3Q25 cited sales and organic sales growth, despite being above 8%, was significantly below marks. It shows you something when even a close to double-digit top-line growth isn't enough to convince analysts or the market. Also, margins missed by about 20 bps - but I don't view this as material enough to have really influenced the trends. Instead, I believe it's all about growth - growth, growth, growth seems to be the mantra that's driving AI trends and markets here, and the companies that don't deliver see not only punishments in share prices, but downgrades in targets.

Market demand in NA came in as more realistic than I expected - though given my experience and expertise in US commercial real estate (office), I could have forecasted that this sector would be problematic. I also don't believe in the recovery of this market that some analysts, and the company, are citing.

One of the company's growth sectors, Data Centers, is also not delivering "according to par". This is not just US-based but EU as well. There are clear implications in key European construction markets that trends are to be more sluggish than expected.

To be clear, I was already pretty forgiving in my last targets for the company. Citing a €100/share PT and a €130-€140 FV implies that the company should be valued on a fair-value basis above 24x P/E. This is despite a low yield of only 1.65% at a price of €133/share.

Legrand is otherwise A-rated, so fairly safe, and with correspondingly low debt. That the company is growing is no doubt - the question is, how do we value this growth or this expected upside, and how do we discount for the issues that we have and see?

So, despite the company citing strong sales growth and net profit, despite 7 M&As, and despite forecasting meeting the July 2025 raised lofty targets in sales growth and profit, the company was punished by the market and has not yet recovered. This tells us something.

It tells us that the results you see above are, in fact, not sufficient to justify the company's valuation. Despite NA delivering over 17% of YoY growth in 9M25, this was insufficient, and characterized as too low. And this is despite Legrand's segment outperforming the rest of the world, and Europe, by a factor of over 8x.

Because I don't believe Europe is likely to provide further growth than we see, and I don't see more coming from the rest of the world either, let me pose the following question; What do you think will happen to the company's rich valuation of over 24-25x P/E when NA stops delivering double-digit growth?

Because I believe we'll see a significant decline and correction in valuation. I believe Legrand is doing everything it can, including using its significant financial "muscles" to provide inorganic growth through the addition of M&As. But I further believe, aside from the risks associated with such a growth strategy, that this will come to an end at some point.

Let me state unequivocally that the company is doing great. From a perspective of profitability, growth, and margins, I believe Legrand is doing a superb job, and management seems to be doing so as well. We can see this in net profit trends.

The challenge is that the company is doing it in a market, and at a valuation that expects more and more, the more the valuation of that market increases.

This is the reason why, for the past 7-8 months, I have been selling holdings. I am now at over 48% cash. I haven't had that much cash since I started investing in stocks well over 10 years ago. The money I hold is currently at a relatively unattractive yield, but I consider this to be favorable to being invested in anything but the most undervalued and conservative companies.

Could I be wrong? Of course I could. Do I believe I am? If I believed that, I wouldn't be sitting on nearly 50% cash. To me, the FOMO (fear of missing out) in this case and context is preferable to the potential of a short-term price drop. Instead, if and when this price drop occurs, I will be in a prime position to take advantage. I should also state very clearly that the reason I am comfortable with this is because I utilize alternative investments in addition to stocks. For about 2 years now, my income and personal wealth have not been dependent on the stock market.

I forecast a 5-6% AEPS growth for Legrand in the 2025 period, citing my EPS growth forecasts for 2025 in line with the company's own expectations. But beyond 2025E, I forecast not a growth of 8-11%, as most seem to, but forecasts in line with the 2025 - "only" about 5-6%.

I further forecast that this will be insufficient to justify the company's market-granted premium valuation of 25-28x P/E, next to the company's historical average. Growth potential in AI and data centers, or inorganic growth, does not change this expectation.

Valuation is as follows.

Valuation for Legrand - Upside only at above 24-25x P/E

In my opinion, Legrand is without a doubt going to keep growing its top and bottom line. However, the current valuation is where expectations meet the realities of the market, and the realities of future potential. Whenever Legrand has been at this premium, the resulting downturn has erased profits at some point, enabling an entry at a lower and more appealing valuation. I have no reason to believe this time will be any different.

An upside in excess of 15% annualized, which is usually what I look for, would require a conservative P/E of above 28.7x, because not even the 28x P/E gives us 15% annualized.

Let me therefore state clearly that Legrand has never managed that sort of upside for any extended period of time (meaning more than 1-2 months). The 20-year average comes to 19x, and the company has at times traded below 10x. At the 20-year average, your forecasted rate of return would be firmly negative without the company's yield, and barely break-even with yield. In any other downturn, you'd be losing money, despite the fact that the company is likely to grow. Forecasting at 19x P/E for 2028E gives us a fair value of about €130/share, which if you recall, also is the fair value that I consider valid for the business. My €100/share PT or below, therefore, retains its validity in my eyes. In November, it was required to see a 15% annualized upside or more, and it still is.

What's changed is that I believe we've gotten more clarity about the risk of overconcentration, of the risk of a downturn if NA demand turns even more muted, and that the market seems to have realized the overvaluation, judging by the clear overreaction to what in all standard metrics was a good 3Q25 and 9M25 period.

I therefore say that my thesis and my PT were correct, and I see no reason to change it despite lofty 2026E expectations. The fact that most analyst targets seem to be at least 14-60% higher than mine does not change my view - or that with €100/share, I'm at least €14/share lower than the lowest S&P Global analyst, which is at €114/share, with an average of about €145/share for 18 analysts.

I reiterate my cautious stance, and would buy only if it were cheaper.

Risks for the company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

I view the company's current operations as safe and management as stellar - fundamentals are good, but the company's valuation prohibits investment. At 25x+, the company is higher than it's been historically. This valuation represents a major risk in a world where the company is punished for even solid results, as we saw in 3Q25, despite them being a miss.

There's significant competition in data centers - Legrand is but one of the players there, and not even the biggest. This results in pricing pressure, which can eat into company margins. Legrand competes with majors like Siemens (SIEGY), which is not easy. I estimate a significant price and margin decline, as these services are set to become more commoditized, not less so.

Furthermore, as a European player aiming at the US market, Legrand faces significant and realistic political and tariff-related risks. Given that almost all of the organic growth comes from NA, this leaves the company vulnerable.

The legacy segments of the company, such as wiring and controls, do not offer a significant advantage to peers either, in my view.

This wouldn't be a problem if the company was cheaper - but due to the high price, I view this as a "no go".

Thesis

Legrand is one of the premier electrical component companies out there. It's A-rated, has managed superb growth (and has been punished despite this growth), and mostly hits its targets and forecasts, except for the latest period, where it missed. The problem remains its valuation, which at over 20% above its 20-year average and 10% above its 5-year average. For 2026, I reiterate previous targets and expectations, citing a PT of €100/share, and a fair value of €130/share - not above this.

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend
  • This company is currently cheap
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a price target of €100/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Commodities: Oil Increasingly Nervous About Potential U.S. Action In Iran
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy and Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

Oil prices continue to move higher as tensions build between the US and Iran amid President Trump's threats to attack.

Energy: Brent breaks above $70/bbl

Oil prices continued their ascent yesterday with ICE Brent rallying 3.38% to settle above US$70/bbl, and its highest level since August. A range of factors is contributing to the move, including broad USD weakness, Kazakh supply disruptions, and developments in the Middle East.

The market is becoming increasingly nervous over potential US action in Iran, with US vessels moving into the region and President Trump threatening an attack if Iran doesn’t make a deal regarding its nuclear programme.

Given Trump’s recent rhetoric, one would have to be fairly brave to head into the weekend short the market. Iran’s foreign minister will be visiting Turkey today, with the Turkish government offering to mediate talks between the US and Iran.

The oil market is also tighter than many had expected. The strength in the time spreads is at odds with expectations of a large surplus. The forward curve has moved deeper into backwardation this month, with the curve backwardated all the way through to the Aug-27 contract.

Meanwhile, the 12-month ICE Brent spread is trading at $5/bbl. This is the strongest level since June 2025, when we last saw US attacks on Iranian nuclear infrastructure. Disruptions to Kazakh oil flows have certainly added to market tightness.

There’s been a significant reduction in loadings at the CPC terminal, while repair work was carried out due to drone attacks towards the end of last year. Yet with this repair work complete, loadings should normalise, helping to ease some of the tightness.

Another factor contributing to a tighter market than the balance sheet shows includes reduced appetite among some refiners to buy Russian oil amid US sanctions and the EU ban on refined product imports from Russian oil. The latter would see some refiners, particularly in India, choose to process non-Russian oil.

This is to ensure they can continue selling refined products into the EU. The difficulty Russia has in finding willing buyers is reflected in the large volume of Russian oil floating at sea. Generally, growing storage is considered a bearish factor. Yet if there are limited willing buyers for this oil, it might not be so bearish after all.

European natural gas prices also rallied yesterday, with TTF settling more than 4% higher. The European gas market has rallied this month amid tighter storage levels, colder weather, and the severe winter storm in the US. However, developments between Iran and the US will provide a further boost.

Escalation between the US and Iran puts a significant portion of LNG supply at risk. Qatar, the second-largest exporter, ships volumes through the Strait of Hormuz. Qatar accounted for approximately 19% of global LNG export volumes in 2025.

In the US, the latest storage data from the EIA shows that natural gas storage fell by 242Bcf over the last week. This is relatively aligned with market expectations. It leaves total storage 7.9% higher year-on-year and 5.3% above the 5-year average. Given that this data covers through 23 January, we will need to wait for next week’s release, which will reflect the impact of the recent winter storm.

Metals: Copper surges past $14,500/t in a volatile session

Copper soared above $14,500/t for the first time on Thursday. It marked the largest one-day gain in more than 16 years during an exceptionally volatile trading session. The early surge, driven largely during Asian hours, coincided with a spike in activity across China’s exchanges. This helped propel both LME and Comex prices to fresh record highs. LME prices gained as much as 11%, before the market later gave back part of those gains.

The afternoon pullback reflected a broader shift into risk-off positioning, with global equity markets under pressure and the US dollar moving higher. Other metals on the LME followed a similar pattern, rallying sharply in the morning only to retreat later in the session.

This volatility comes amid a powerful, broad-based rally across the metals complex since the start of the year. Investors are piling into the sector as fundamentals tighten. Copper in London has risen about 21% since the start of December.

Despite the sharp intraday swings, the underlying backdrop for copper and the wider metals complex remains constructive. Expectations of a weaker dollar, anticipated rate cuts, firm investor appetite for real assets, and persistently tight supply-demand conditions continue to provide support.

Gold and silver also reversed sharply after setting fresh records earlier in the session. Spot gold climbed above $5,595/oz, while silver briefly surged past $120/oz, before both came sharply lower. In this environment, gold is increasingly being used as a source of liquidity rather than a traditional safe-haven asset.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Cadence Design Systems: Riding The AI Supercycle, But With Expectations At The Limit
data.description:
Cadence Design Systems (CDNS) is clearly well placed to ride the rising complexity of chip and system design in the AI age. The need for advanced electronic design automation (EDA), verification tools, and high-performance IP continues to expand. Cadence has a record backlog to show alongside very healthy margins and cash flow. The business quality and execution is an easier thesis to write - it should be a Buy.

The nuance in Cadence's thesis is not about the business's quality, but about the fact that most of the quality is already appreciated by the markets, and current valuations at ~40x price to forward earnings leave little room for rerating. From here on, fresh investments will have to rely on durable compounding over the next several years, proving this demand is structural and not cyclical and can be sustained at mid-teens for the foreseeable future.

The odds of that compounding scenario are higher, no doubt, as the AI tailwinds appear durable. But the reward of going right is just double digit compounding, assuming Cadence grows into these lofty valuations over time. On the flip side, the magnitude of downside risks is far higher, although their odds aren't. However, in the event of any negative surprises, Cadence could get pressured by not only a pullback in earnings but also a rerating that could be more severe than the upside offers. This tension is more applicable because much of the transformative gains look like they are already embedded today in the share prices. Hence, Cadence is best rated a Hold, with a wait for fresh triggers that could open up asymmetric upside odds.

The Odds of A Continued Boom

The business quality and the odds of maintaining a mid-teens growth are extremely high. Verification is now an exponential task in chip design, which means even if semiconductor volumes slow down, complexity could be enough to support Cadence's EDA growth. Cadence is monetizing the AI tailwinds at multiple layers beyond EDA. AI-enabled tools like Cerebrus and SimAI are already delivering 4x productivity improvement and 5x-10x improvement in verification throughput. Growth in design IP is being fueled by interconnect protocols driven by AI and chiplet architectures. Demand for Cadence’s hardware machines is also strong because today’s AI chips are so large and complex that they must be tested on massive simulation systems before they can work in the real world. Cadence's positioning is therefore getting supported across multiple layers of the AI infrastructure buildout.

Near-term demand visibility is extremely strong. In Q3 2025, Cadence reported a backlog of over $7b - broad based across segments. Backlog in EDA is typically tied to multi-year agreements. Revenue growth guidance for the full year is still a healthy 14%, with a strong non-GAAP operating margin in the mid-40s.

Trends in Headline Metrics

While growth levels guided for the full-year look healthy, the topline growth acceleration has actually peaked in Q4 2024. Q3 2025 growth was barely double digits. The 14% growth guided for the full year translates to 5-6% topline growth YoY in Q4, showing a worrying trend in growth deceleration.

GAAP operating margins are still improving. Margin improvements in the face of moderating revenue growth suggest pricing discipline, a favorable mix, and the fact that operating leverage is still intact. If growth does not rebound, mid-teens EPS growth will have to come from three levers working together.

One of the factors Cadence's EPS growth relies on is sustained favorable product mix. Higher-margin segments like software and IP should continue to grow faster than the corporate average. This is not unlikely given management's optimism around a favorable mix driven by IP growth -

I would be surprised if our IP business does not grow better than Cadence average, which it should, given the profitability profile. We want that to happen. If the profitability is slightly lower than EDA, then the growth should be higher than Cadence average.

The second factor that could help margins is improved pricing power that tools like Cerebrus and SimAI can offer, once the productivity gains become meaningful levers. The third factor that could support EPS is continued strong free cash flow generation that supports a strong buyback policy. The free cash flow today accounts for around a third of the buybacks, and it has been even more aggressively used in the past to support share prices and mechanically improve EPS.

Although the odds of the EPS supportive cash flow generation and margin improvements are still high, the work ahead looks tougher if revenue does not rebound strongly in 2026. At the moment we have strong revenue visibility in the form of a solid backlog, but the timing mix will dictate near-term revenue realization. While hardware and IP revenues are upfront in nature, software revenues can be recognized more slowly, often through a contract's duration. I do not see any concerns around the revenue growth slowing to levels that put EPS growth under threat in 2026, but the margin for error is clearly narrowing. Even near term and temporary earnings misses could very well pressure valuations that are pricing for near perfection.

Upside Math Tough

At ~40x forward earnings, Cadence is already priced like a durable compounder. The stock has been absorbing EPS growth without material rerating over the past couple of years. If Cadence keeps delivering mid-teens EPS growth over the next three years (as embedded expectations show), valuations would still be a high ~34x forward earnings if the stock does not move.

In such an environment of low rerating possibilities, most of the heavy lifting is expected to be shouldered by earnings growth. This is very much probable, but assumes, high valuations are maintained for a very long time. The sensitivity of high valuations may not be tested if earnings keep up pace. But the risks at these levels are that any temporary variance in EPS is sufficient to test multiples. If EPS growth drifts toward high single digits in any of the upcoming 2-3 quarters, that temporary setback could be sufficient for multiple compression. In other words, Cadence's risks are exposed to minor expectation mismatches and do not need material business deterioration to get triggered. Usual risks like export control changes, or China demand normalization, would have been easier to digest at lower valuations, but they become material risks - with a lot of downside opening up if any of them materialize.

Every quarter becomes important to watch from here on, and Q4 is no exception. I will be watching primarily for signals around whether growth is stabilizing or decelerating further. One of the two core expectations needs to be met in Q4: continued backlog expansion and topline confidence, or IP growth and favorable mix shifts improving operating margins. What could trigger fresh Buys in my mind are signs of reacceleration in revenue exiting Q4. Any pricing uplift signals could be supportive too. If the EPS shows promise of breaching the mid-teens expectations, either through topline momentum or better than expected margins, I think the valuation will get fresh impetus to rerate (or at least stay supported). That is a better regime to enter fresh positions in Cadence. Not in an environment of high odds of just meeting expectations.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 01/30/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.
